title: Figma comp → build
subtitle: The universal handoff
useWhen: Translating any Figma design into working HTML. Use every time.
body:
[Attach Figma screenshot]
Build this as a single HTML file. Match the design closely.

Constraints:
- Use my tokens [paste]
- Use these fonts: [Google Fonts links]
- Visual direction: "[name]" — [3-word feel]
- Banned moves: [paste relevant items]

If something in the comp seems unusual, don't smooth it out — ask me about it. Unusual is often the point.
Don't add sections that aren't in my comp. Don't add "improvements".

title: Voice match
useWhen: Rewriting AI-generated copy to sound like you wrote it.
body:
Here's how I write: [paste 1–2 paragraphs of your own writing — old emails, journal, anything authentic].

Now write [the new piece] in this voice. Then tell me what choices you made to match — sentence rhythm, word choices, what you avoided.

Don't write more than I asked for. Don't add a polite intro or outro.

title: Set up a Claude Project
useWhen: Starting a new ongoing work area where Claude needs context.
body:
Help me design a Claude Project for [recurring work area, e.g. "design critiques" or "case study writing"].

Walk through:
1. What custom instructions should the project have? (tone, role, default response structure)
2. What reference files should I attach? (style guides, examples of good output, banned-words lists)
3. What should the project ALWAYS ask for before responding?
4. What should it never do?

Draft the custom instructions in full. Then list the files I should attach and why each one helps.

title: Use Planning mode
subtitle: Extended thinking
useWhen: Complex problems where you'd rather have a sharp answer than a fast one.
body:
[Toggle on Extended thinking / Planning mode in Claude before sending this]

I have a complex problem: [describe situation in detail].

Before answering, think through this thoroughly. Consider multiple angles, edge cases, and what could go wrong. I'd rather you take longer and give me a sharper answer than rush to a quick one.

Specifically work through:
1. What are the actual constraints (not the stated ones)?
2. What are 3 different approaches and their tradeoffs?
3. What's the answer most people give that I should NOT take?
4. What's your actual recommendation and why?

title: Build a custom Skill
useWhen: Recurring task you want Claude to handle the same way every time.
body:
I want to create a Claude Skill (an .md file with instructions Claude reads automatically when relevant) for [recurring task].

Walk me through what should go in SKILL.md:
1. The exact trigger conditions (when should this skill activate?)
2. The instructions Claude follows when triggered
3. Reference files or templates to bundle
4. Common failure modes to guard against

Skills differ from Projects: Projects hold context for ongoing work, Skills are reusable techniques that activate automatically when conditions match. Draft the SKILL.md now.

title: Use Claude as research operator
useWhen: Researching something current. Claude needs to search, not pull from memory.
body:
I need to research [topic] thoroughly. Don't give me a summary from memory — actually search the web for current information.

Specifically:
1. Find 5–7 high-quality sources from the last 12 months
2. Identify where sources disagree, not just where they agree
3. Flag anything that contradicts conventional wisdom
4. Note gaps — what would I want to know that you couldn't find?

Cite sources with links. Be skeptical of marketing content. I'd rather have 3 great sources than 10 mediocre ones.

title: Multi-shot example prompting
useWhen: Teaching Claude a pattern by showing it, before asking it to repeat.
body:
I want you to do [task] consistently. Before I give you a real one to do, here are 3 examples of how I want it done:

EXAMPLE 1: Input: [example input] | Output: [example output]
EXAMPLE 2: Input: [example input] | Output: [example output]
EXAMPLE 3: Input: [example input] | Output: [example output]

What pattern do you see in these? Articulate the rules in your own words. Then I'll give you a real one to apply them to.

title: Component variations
subtitle: Taste training
useWhen: Want to see a range of options for the same component, intentionally distinct.
body:
Build me 3 versions of [component] — one minimal, one expressive, one playful. Same content in all three. All feel intentional, not dialed-up versions of each other.

Banned moves: [paste]. Tokens: [paste].

After you build them, tell me which one you'd ship and why. Then I'll tell you which one I'd ship and we'll see where we disagree.

title: Critique my taste
subtitle: Not my work
useWhen: Want to know what your taste actually looks like as a pattern.
body:
[Attach 5–10 screenshots of work you've recently saved or admired]

Look at these as a set. What does my taste look like? What patterns appear? What am I drawn to that I might not have articulated?

Then: where is my taste narrow? What kind of work am I probably under-exposed to that would expand my range? Suggest 5 designers/studios I should look at to push my edges.

title: Reverse-engineer a design
useWhen: Found a design you love. Want to articulate what makes it work.
body:
[Attach screenshot of a design you admire]

Don't summarize what's in this design — articulate what makes it visually distinctive. Tell me:
1. What it does that most designs DON'T do
2. The specific design decisions (type weight relationships, spacing rhythm, color proportions, what's deliberately missing)
3. What I could borrow as a technique (vs what's signature to them and would feel like cribbing)
4. What it tells me about the designer's worldview

title: Accessibility quick check
useWhen: Built something. Want a fast accessibility review with specific fixes.
body:
Review this HTML/CSS for accessibility issues. Focus on:
1. Contrast ratios — give me actual numbers, not "looks fine"
2. Semantic HTML — is this using divs where it should use buttons, lists, sections?
3. Focus states — visible? Distinct from hover?
4. Keyboard navigation — can every interactive element be reached and operated?
5. Screen reader basics — alt text, ARIA where needed, labels on inputs

List issues with specific fixes. No general advice. No "ensure good contrast" — give me hex values that fail and what to change them to.

title: Killing AI tells
useWhen: Cleaning AI smell out of a draft.
body:
Here's a draft: [paste].

Identify the 5 phrases that scream 'AI wrote this' — the LinkedIn-isms, the "in today's fast-paced world", the "navigating the landscape", the "leveraging". Rewrite each in plain human language.

Show before and after for each. Then give me a quick rule for how I'd catch these myself in future drafts.

title: Microcopy generator
useWhen: Need to write a small piece of UI copy with personality.
body:
I need microcopy for [specific UI moment, e.g. "an empty state for a project list when the user hasn't created any projects yet"].

Generate 5 versions across this range:
- Functional (just tells you what to do)
- Friendly (warm but not cute)
- Wry (a small joke that respects the user)
- Encouraging (gentle motivation)
- Editorial (sounds like a thoughtful publication)

Maximum 12 words per option. Banned: "Oops!", "Looks like…", "Whoops!", any exclamation marks, any emojis.

title: Case study skeleton
useWhen: Writing up a project and don't know where to start.
body:
Help me write a case study about [project]. I'll tell you the basics, you ask the right follow-ups.

Start by asking: what was the actual problem (not the project brief)? What did the team initially get wrong? What surprised you?

Build the case study in 4 sections: Context (what was the situation), Tension (what was hard about this), Move (what we actually did), Outcome (what happened, including what didn't work).

Avoid: "I'm passionate about", "user-centric", "leveraged", "drove engagement". Be specific about what we did, not what we believed in.

title: 5-perspectives critique
useWhen: Want pushback on something from multiple angles, not just one.
body:
Here's [the thing]. Critique it from 5 different perspectives, one at a time:
1. A skeptical user with no context
2. A senior designer who's seen everything
3. An accessibility specialist
4. A copywriter
5. Someone who'd never use this product

For each perspective, give me 2 specific issues, not generalities.

title: Steel-manning my idea
useWhen: About to commit to a decision. Want the strongest case against.
body: I'm considering [decision]. Argue the strongest case AGAINST what I'm planning. Don't be polite. What am I missing? What would a smart person who disagrees with me say?

title: Make a decision you'll defend
useWhen: Stuck between options. Want a structured way through it.
body:
I'm trying to decide between [option A] and [option B] for [context].

Don't tell me which to pick. Instead:
1. What 3–4 questions about MYSELF should I answer first?
2. For each question, how would a "yes" point me one way vs. the other?
3. Imagine it's 6 months from now and this decision failed completely. Walk me through the most likely failure story for each option.
4. Based on those stories: which wrong is more recoverable?

Help me see the decision more clearly. Don't make it for me.

title: Project scope sniff test
useWhen: About to commit to a project. Want a reality check on scope.
body:
Here's what I'm planning to build: [describe project + timeline].

Sanity check the scope:
1. What's likely to take 3× longer than I think?
2. What am I assuming will be easy that won't be?
3. What's the smallest version of this that would still be worth shipping?
4. What's the riskiest assumption I haven't tested?
5. If I had half the time, what would I cut first?

Be skeptical. I'd rather hear it now than at the deadline.

title: Weekly review partner
useWhen: End of the week. Want to actually review instead of drifting.
body:
It's the end of the week. Help me review.

Ask me:
1. What did I actually finish? (specific things, not "made progress on X")
2. What did I plan to finish but didn't, and why?
3. What did I learn that changes how I'd plan next week?
4. What energy am I bringing into next week — depleted, neutral, energized?

Then help me pick the 3 most important things for next week. Not 10. Three.

title: Break down a vague goal
useWhen: Have an aspiration but no plan. Want to translate it into something real.
body:
My goal is [vague aspiration, e.g. "get better at motion design"].

Break this down:
1. What does "better" actually mean here? Give me 3 ways to measure it.
2. What would a 4-week plan look like to make visible progress on one of those measures?
3. What's the smallest weekly commitment that would actually move the needle?
4. What should I deliberately ignore that I'd be tempted to chase?

Be specific. "Practice more" is not an answer.

title: Difficult message draft
useWhen: Need to write a message you've been putting off.
body:
I need to write [type of message — e.g. "an email pushing back on a deadline" / "Slack message saying I disagree with the direction"] to [recipient and their relationship to me].

Help me draft 3 versions:
1. Direct — clear, no softening, professionally honest
2. Warm — same content but with relationship care
3. Strategic — leads with what they need to hear before what I need to say

For each, tell me what I'm trading off. Then I'll pick which to send.

title: Translate frustration into feedback
useWhen: Frustrated about something. Want to respond well, not vent.
body:
Here's something I'm frustrated about: [vent freely, no filter].

Turn this into feedback that's:
1. Actionable (the recipient knows what to change)
2. Specific (refers to actual events, not patterns)
3. Owns my part (not "you always X" but "when X happened, I felt Y")
4. Respectful (assumes good intent)

Give me the message I should actually send. Not the one that vents the feeling.

title: Disagree without conflict
useWhen: Need to push back on someone whose position deserves real engagement.
body:
I disagree with [person] about [decision]. Their position is [their argument]. My position is [your argument].

Help me write a response that:
1. Genuinely engages with their strongest point (not a strawman)
2. States my disagreement clearly without being combative
3. Offers a path forward — either "let's run a small test" or "here's what would change my mind" or "let's escalate to X"

Don't make it diplomatic to the point of being unclear. Disagreement should be readable.
